question: how does the soil food web work
title: How Does the Soil Food Web Work? The Underground Engine of Plant Growth
metaDescription: The soil food web is the living network of organisms that drives nutrient cycling, plant growth, and soil health. Here's how it works — from bacteria to earthworms — and why every gardener needs to understand it.
content:
# How Does the Soil Food Web Work? The Underground Engine of Plant Growth

Most people think plants get their nutrients from fertilizer. Or maybe from rain. The truth is more interesting, and a lot more alive. Plants get most of their nutrients from a web of organisms living in the soil around their roots — bacteria, fungi, nematodes, protozoa, arthropods, earthworms — all interacting with each other and with plant roots in a system so complex it makes the Amazon rainforest look simple.

The USDA's Natural Resources Conservation Service calls it the soil food web. I call it the underground engine. And once you understand how it works, you'll never look at a garden bed the same way again.

## Start at the Bottom: Bacteria and Fungi

Every food web has a base. In soil, that base is the decomposers — primarily bacteria and fungi.

Bacteria are the workhorses. A single teaspoon of healthy soil can contain anywhere from 100 million to a billion bacteria, representing thousands of species. They colonize the surfaces of soil particles and plant roots. They consume organic matter — dead leaves, root exudates, compost — and break it down into simpler compounds. Faster-cycling bacteria tend to dominate in disturbed soils with simple organic inputs.

Fungi do something different and equally critical. They extend long thread-like structures called hyphae through the soil, forming networks that can span many feet. These networks — mycorrhizal fungi in particular — physically connect to plant roots and extend the plant's reach far beyond what roots alone could achieve. In exchange for sugars the plant produces through photosynthesis, fungi deliver water, phosphorus, and other minerals from distant parts of the soil profile. This is one of the oldest partnerships in plant biology, going back hundreds of millions of years.

Fungi also break down the tougher stuff: lignin, cellulose, the woody materials that bacteria struggle with. Their hyphae physically bind soil particles together into aggregates, creating the crumbly structure that lets water infiltrate and roots penetrate. Healthy fungal networks are a primary indicator of living soil.

## The Grazers: Protozoa and Nematodes

Bacteria and fungi store nutrients in their cells. The system for getting those nutrients into a form plants can use is called predation — and it starts with the grazers.

Protozoa are single-celled organisms that engulf and digest bacteria whole. When a protozoan eats a bacterium, it takes in more nitrogen than it needs. The excess nitrogen gets excreted into the soil as ammonium — one of the most directly plant-available forms of nitrogen there is. This is the decay cycle at work: nitrogen locked inside microbial cells gets released into the soil solution every time a protozoan eats.

Nematodes are tiny roundworms — some feed on bacteria, some on fungi, some on other nematodes. Bacterial-feeding nematodes work the same way protozoa do: they eat nitrogen-rich microbes and excrete the excess as plant-available nutrients. Fungal-feeding nematodes do the same for the fungal channel of the food web.

The NRCS Soil Biology Primer documents this process clearly: as organisms in the soil consume each other and consume organic matter, nutrients and energy move through the food web and become available to plants. It's not fertilizer that feeds plants — it's biology.

## Predators and Engineers: Arthropods and Earthworms

Higher up the food web, arthropods — springtails, mites, beetles, centipedes — prey on nematodes, fungi, and bacteria. They fragment organic matter into smaller pieces, which increases the surface area available to microbial decomposers. Every time an arthropod chews on a dead leaf or breaks apart a piece of compost, it accelerates the decay cycle.

Earthworms are the engineers of the soil food web. They're not really decomposers themselves — they primarily consume bacteria, fungi, and partially decomposed organic matter as they move through the soil. But what they leave behind is transformative. Earthworm castings are among the most nutrient-rich materials in any soil system, loaded with plant-available minerals, beneficial bacteria, and humus. Their burrows create channels for water and air movement. Their movement breaks compaction and mixes layers.

A healthy soil system has earthworms. Heavily tilled, pesticide-treated, or compacted soil often has very few. The presence or absence of earthworms is one of the most reliable indicators of soil health that a gardener can observe without any equipment.

## The Rhizosphere: Where It All Connects to Plants

The soil food web doesn't operate uniformly throughout the soil profile. It's concentrated most densely in a zone called the rhizosphere — the millimeters of soil directly surrounding plant roots.

Plants don't just passively receive nutrients from soil. They actively shape their root zone. Through their roots, plants release exudates — sugars, amino acids, organic acids — that feed specific types of bacteria and fungi. The plant essentially farms its own microbial community, feeding the organisms that will in turn provide the nutrients it needs.

This is one of the most remarkable facts in plant biology: plants can selectively cultivate bacteria that solubilize phosphorus, or fungi that deliver water during drought, or organisms that suppress specific pathogens. The plant communicates with the soil food web through chemistry, and the food web responds.

When you understand this, you understand why soil disturbance is so costly. Every time you till, you sever fungal networks. You disrupt the rhizosphere microbiome. You expose deeper soil to conditions it's not adapted to. The plant has to start the process of building its microbial community from scratch.

## The Decay Cycle Connects It All

The soil food web is really a physical expression of the decay cycle — the loop that turns dead organic matter back into living biomass and plant-available nutrients. Organic matter goes in at the top: leaves, roots, manure, compost. Bacteria and fungi break it down. Protozoa and nematodes consume the bacteria and fungi and release nutrients. Larger predators consume the grazers. Earthworms process the whole system. And at each step, nutrients that were locked in complex organic forms get simplified into forms that plant roots can absorb.

Gabe Brown, one of the regenerative agriculture farmers I pay attention to, talks about this as the biological system that makes soil wealth rather than depleting it. In a functioning soil food web, the output — plant growth, nutrient density, water retention, soil structure — is greater than the direct inputs. The biology creates value that you didn't put in.

Synthetic fertilizers short-circuit this system. They deliver soluble nutrients directly, bypassing the food web. In the short term, plants respond. In the long term, you're starving the biology. Microbial populations decline without the organic matter inputs they need. Fungal networks collapse without the plant partnerships that sustain them. The soil food web diminishes, and with it, the soil's capacity to function.

## How to Feed Your Soil Food Web

You don't manage the soil food web directly — you create the conditions for it to manage itself. A few principles:

**Keep organic matter coming.** Compost, mulch, cover crop residue, leaf litter — all of it feeds the bacteria and fungi at the base of the web. Without organic matter inputs, the whole system runs down.

**Minimize tillage.** Especially deep tillage. Fungal networks are destroyed by mechanical disturbance. Let the earthworms and arthropods do the tillage work; it's slower but it builds structure instead of destroying it.

**Maintain living roots as long as possible.** Plant roots feed the rhizosphere community. When roots die and the soil sits bare, the microbial community around the root zone goes into decline. Cover crops keep roots in the ground through the off-season.

**Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides and fumigants.** These don't just kill pests — they kill soil organisms indiscriminately. The collateral damage to the food web can take years to recover from.

**Diversify what you grow.** Different plants cultivate different microbial communities. A diverse planting creates a diverse soil food web, which is more resilient and more capable than a monoculture soil.

Y'all, your plants have an underground partner network that's been doing this work for hundreds of millions of years. Your job is mostly to keep feeding it and get out of the way.

---
